content.text: The Chunking Size Dilemma

Choosing a chunk size for RAG forces a trade-off. Small chunks (50–150 tokens) embed precise, focused concepts and match queries with high precision — but they strip away surrounding context, so the retrieved passage may be too narrow for the LLM to generate a grounded answer. Large chunks (400–1000 tokens) provide rich context but their embeddings average over many ideas, reducing recall for specific queries. Parent-child chunking resolves this by separating the retrieval unit from the generation unit: small child chunks are used for retrieval precision, and their larger parent chunks are returned to the LLM for context richness.

attrs.alt: Parent-child chunking: small child chunks embedded in vector store, parent chunks stored separately and fetched at generation time
attrs.title: Parent-Child / Hierarchical Chunking

content.text: How ParentDocumentRetriever Works

LangChain's ParentDocumentRetriever implements the parent-child pattern with three components: a text splitter for parent chunks, a child splitter for child chunks, a vector store that holds child embeddings, and a document store that holds the full parent chunks. At index time, parents are stored in the doc store and their children are embedded and stored in the vector store with a parent_id metadata reference. At query time, the retriever searches the vector store to find matching children, then fetches the full parent chunk from the doc store for each match.

from langchain.retrievers import ParentDocumentRetriever
from langchain.storage import InMemoryStore
from langchain_community.vectorstores import Chroma
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings
from langchain_text_splitters import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter

embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings()

# Parent splitter: large chunks returned to LLM
parent_splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(chunk_size=512, chunk_overlap=50)

# Child splitter: small chunks for precise embedding retrieval
child_splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(chunk_size=100, chunk_overlap=10)

# Vector store holds only child embeddings
vectorstore = Chroma(embedding_function=embeddings)

# Doc store holds the full parent chunks (in-memory for dev; use Redis/Postgres in prod)
docstore = InMemoryStore()

retriever = ParentDocumentRetriever(
    vectorstore=vectorstore,
    docstore=docstore,
    child_splitter=child_splitter,
    parent_splitter=parent_splitter,
)

retriever.add_documents(docs)  # indexes parents + children automatically

# Query returns full parent chunks, not the tiny child matches
results = retriever.invoke("how does attention mechanism compute weights")
for doc in results:
    print(len(doc.page_content), doc.page_content[:80])
Full Documents vs. Parent Chunks

ParentDocumentRetriever supports two modes. In the first mode (shown above), you provide both a parent_splitter and a child_splitter: the document is split into mid-size parent chunks, and each parent is further split into child chunks for indexing. In the second mode, you omit parent_splitter: the entire original document is the parent, and child chunks are small windows into it. The second mode is better when documents are naturally scoped (e.g., one article = one coherent topic) and you want the LLM to see the full document context whenever any part of it is retrieved.

# Mode 2: full document as parent, child chunks for retrieval only
full_doc_retriever = ParentDocumentRetriever(
    vectorstore=Chroma(embedding_function=embeddings),
    docstore=InMemoryStore(),
    child_splitter=RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(chunk_size=150, chunk_overlap=20),
    # No parent_splitter → whole document is the parent
)

full_doc_retriever.add_documents(docs)
results = full_doc_retriever.invoke("transformer positional encoding")
# Returns complete documents — can be thousands of tokens each
Persistent Storage for Production

InMemoryStore is useful for prototyping but does not survive process restarts. In production, replace it with a persistent key-value store. LangChain provides RedisStore and SQLiteEntityStore; you can also wrap any dict-like store using the BaseStore interface. The vector store (Chroma, Pinecone, Weaviate) provides its own persistence separately.

import redis
from langchain.storage import RedisStore
from langchain_community.vectorstores import Chroma

# Production: Redis for parent doc storage + persistent Chroma
redis_client = redis.Redis(host="localhost", port=6379, db=0)
docstore = RedisStore(client=redis_client, namespace="rag:parents")

vectorstore = Chroma(
    persist_directory="./chroma_db",
    embedding_function=embeddings,
)

retriever = ParentDocumentRetriever(
    vectorstore=vectorstore,
    docstore=docstore,
    child_splitter=RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(chunk_size=100, chunk_overlap=10),
    parent_splitter=RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(chunk_size=512, chunk_overlap=50),
)

# Re-indexing is safe — add_documents with the same doc IDs overwrites existing entries
retriever.add_documents(docs, ids=[doc.metadata["id"] for doc in docs])
Multi-Level Hierarchies

Some corpora benefit from three levels: section (1000+ tokens), paragraph (200–400 tokens), and sentence (50–100 tokens). The standard ParentDocumentRetriever only supports two levels, but you can chain retrievers or build a custom hierarchy. A common approach is to store sentence-level child chunks in the vector store, map them to paragraph-level parents in the doc store, and expose the paragraph to the LLM — keeping context rich but not overwhelming.

from langchain_text_splitters import SentenceTransformersTokenTextSplitter

# Three-level manual hierarchy
section_splitter   = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(chunk_size=1000, chunk_overlap=100)
paragraph_splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(chunk_size=300, chunk_overlap=30)
sentence_splitter  = SentenceTransformersTokenTextSplitter(chunk_overlap=0, tokens_per_chunk=64)

def build_hierarchy(docs, vectorstore, docstore):
    for doc in docs:
        sections = section_splitter.create_documents(
            [doc.page_content], metadatas=[doc.metadata]
        )
        for section in sections:
            section_id = str(hash(section.page_content))
            docstore.mset([(section_id, section)])  # store section as parent

            paragraphs = paragraph_splitter.create_documents(
                [section.page_content],
                metadatas=[{**section.metadata, "parent_id": section_id}]
            )
            for para in paragraphs:
                para_id = str(hash(para.page_content))
                docstore.mset([(para_id, para)])

                sentences = sentence_splitter.create_documents(
                    [para.page_content],
                    metadatas=[{**para.metadata, "parent_id": para_id}]
                )
                # Embed sentences; store paragraph_id for lookup
                vectorstore.add_documents(sentences)
When to Use Parent-Child Chunking

Parent-child chunking is most valuable when your documents are dense and structured — technical documentation, research papers, legal contracts — where a single concept spans multiple paragraphs but a query targets one precise point in the text. It is less effective on short-form content (FAQs, bullet-point lists) where chunks are already small and context-complete. It also adds indexing complexity: you must manage two storage systems and keep them in sync during updates and deletions.
Scenario
Recommended approach
Dense technical docs (APIs, papers)
Parent-child: child ~100 tokens, parent ~512 tokens
Short FAQ entries
Flat chunking — each entry is already self-contained
Long narrative documents (books, reports)
Full-doc mode: child for retrieval, whole doc as parent
Multi-topic articles
Three-level hierarchy: sentence → paragraph → section
Incremental Updates and Deletion

Managing updates in a two-store system requires care. When a source document changes, you must delete its children from the vector store, remove its parent from the doc store, and re-index both. The record_manager pattern in LangChain tracks which documents have been indexed with a source hash; re-indexing with the same source ID automatically cleans up stale children and parents without full re-indexing. This is critical for corpora that update frequently — product documentation, knowledge bases, or policy repositories.

from langchain.indexes import SQLRecordManager, index

# Set up a record manager backed by SQLite (tracks doc hashes + IDs)
record_manager = SQLRecordManager(
    namespace="chroma/rag_docs",
    db_url="sqlite:///record_manager.db",
)
record_manager.create_schema()

# Incremental indexing: only re-processes changed docs, cleans up deleted ones
result = index(
    docs_source,          # list[Document] from your loader
    record_manager,
    vectorstore,
    cleanup="incremental",  # "full" for complete replacement, "incremental" for delta
    source_id_key="source",  # metadata key that uniquely identifies the source file
)

print(result)
# {'num_added': 5, 'num_updated': 2, 'num_skipped': 18, 'num_deleted': 1}
Measuring Retrieval Quality: Precision vs Context Coverage

Evaluating parent-child retrieval requires two separate metrics. Precision@k on child chunks tells you whether the retrieval step found the right passage; context coverage on parent chunks tells you whether the returned context contains enough information to answer the question. A retriever can have high child precision but still fail if the parent chunk cuts off the relevant answer. Use RAGAS faithfulness and answer relevancy scores to jointly evaluate both dimensions.

# Evaluate parent-child retrieval with RAGAS
# pip install ragas
from ragas import evaluate
from ragas.metrics import faithfulness, answer_relevancy, context_precision
from datasets import Dataset

eval_data = {
    "question": ["how does attention work", "what is positional encoding"],
    "answer": [generated_answer_1, generated_answer_2],
    "contexts": [
        [doc.page_content for doc in retriever.invoke("how does attention work")],
        [doc.page_content for doc in retriever.invoke("what is positional encoding")],
    ],
    "ground_truth": ["correct answer 1", "correct answer 2"],
}

dataset = Dataset.from_dict(eval_data)
scores = evaluate(dataset, metrics=[faithfulness, answer_relevancy, context_precision])
print(scores)
# {'faithfulness': 0.92, 'answer_relevancy': 0.88, 'context_precision': 0.85}
Overlap Strategy and Chunk Boundaries

Both parent and child splitters accept a chunk_overlap parameter. Overlap ensures that information near chunk boundaries is captured by adjacent chunks, preventing key context from being silently truncated at a split point. For parent chunks, 50–100 token overlap is standard; for child chunks, 10–20 tokens is sufficient since precision rather than coverage is the goal. Setting overlap too high wastes embedding capacity on repeated content; setting it to zero risks splitting mid-sentence.

from langchain_text_splitters import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter

# Recommended overlap settings for typical RAG documents
parent_splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(
    chunk_size=512,
    chunk_overlap=50,          # ~10% overlap — enough to bridge sentence boundaries
    separators=["\n\n", "\n", ". ", " "],  # prefer paragraph > line > sentence > word
)

child_splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(
    chunk_size=100,
    chunk_overlap=15,          # just enough to avoid mid-phrase cuts
    separators=["\n", ". ", " "],
)

# Verify chunk distribution after splitting
sample_doc = docs[0]
parents = parent_splitter.create_documents([sample_doc.page_content])
children_per_parent = [
    len(child_splitter.create_documents([p.page_content])) for p in parents
]
print(f"Parents: {len(parents)}, Children/parent: {children_per_parent}")
# Parents: 4, Children/parent: [5, 6, 4, 5]
Combining with Hybrid Retrieval

Parent-child chunking composes naturally with hybrid (BM25 + dense) retrieval. The BM25 retriever operates on child chunks for keyword matching, while the dense retriever operates on child embeddings for semantic matching. Both return child chunk IDs; the fusion step (RRF or score-weighted merge) merges and deduplicates them, then the doc store fetch maps each surviving child ID back to its parent chunk. The result is a set of semantically and lexically relevant parent chunks ready for the LLM context window.

from langchain.retrievers import ParentDocumentRetriever, EnsembleRetriever
from langchain_community.retrievers import BM25Retriever

# Build child chunk list (needed for BM25 over children only)
child_docs = []
for doc in docs:
    child_docs.extend(child_splitter.create_documents([doc.page_content]))

bm25_child = BM25Retriever.from_documents(child_docs)
bm25_child.k = 10

# Dense retriever also targets child embeddings
dense_child = vectorstore.as_retriever(search_kwargs={"k": 10})

# Fuse at the child level, then map to parents
ensemble_child = EnsembleRetriever(
    retrievers=[bm25_child, dense_child],
    weights=[0.4, 0.6],
)

def hybrid_parent_retriever(query: str, top_n: int = 5):
    child_results = ensemble_child.invoke(query)
    # Fetch parents via doc store
    parent_ids = [doc.metadata.get("parent_id") for doc in child_results]
    seen = set()
    parents = []
    for pid in parent_ids:
        if pid and pid not in seen:
            seen.add(pid)
            parent = docstore.mget([pid])[0]
            if parent:
                parents.append(parent)
        if len(parents) >= top_n:
            break
    return parents
Common Pitfalls

Several issues appear frequently when deploying parent-child retrieval. Forgetting to store parent_id in child metadata is the most common: without it, child results cannot be mapped back to parents and the retriever silently returns nothing. Using the same chunk size for both parent and child splitters defeats the purpose — the child size must be meaningfully smaller (at least 3–5× smaller). Finally, over-fragmenting with very small child chunks (under 40 tokens) produces embeddings that are too narrow to capture any semantic signal and degrade retrieval quality below flat chunking.
Always set
parent_id
in child metadata — ParentDocumentRetriever does this automatically, manual implementations must do it explicitly.
Child chunk size < 40 tokens degrades embedding quality. Target 80–150 tokens for child chunks.
Log child-to-parent ratio after indexing. A ratio above 20 children per parent indicates parent chunks are too large and the context may exceed the LLM's context window.
When updating documents, delete both the child vectors and the parent doc store entries — partial cleanup leaves orphaned records that inflate retrieval noise.
Summary
Index small child chunks for retrieval precision; return large parent chunks for generation context.
LangChain
ParentDocumentRetriever
handles the two-level parent-child pattern with a vector store + doc store pair.
Replace
InMemoryStore
with RedisStore or a SQL-backed store for production persistence.
Three-level hierarchies (sentence/paragraph/section) require custom indexing logic beyond the built-in retriever.
Best suited for dense, long-form documents where semantic precision and contextual richness are both required.
